coredata.dc:title: Reduced carbon nanodots as a novel substrate for direct analysis of bisphenol analogs in surface assisted laser desorption/ionization time of flight mass spectrometry
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Wenjing
Li, Ruijin
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Cai, Zongwei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in a variety of fields owing to the diversified properties. However, the control of the structure and surface function of CDs are still urgent issues to be addressed for using it in specific application. Herein, the reduced state of carbon nanodots (R-CDs) has been produced through a convenient strategy by reduction of the pure carbon dots products, which was utilized as a good candidate of matrix for direct analysis of bisphenol (BP) analogs in negative-ion surface-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (SALDI-TOF MS). Due to the dominant hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs, they were easier to absorb BPs through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions, resulting in highly efficient detection of BPs. Meanwhile, compared with the organic matrix and raw carbon dots, the superior surface structure and properties of this R-CDs surface provide various advantages including low background noise, outstanding sensitivity and salt tolerance. This work opens a door for developing R-CDs as alternative substrate in SALDI-TOF MS for direct detection of small molecule pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Reduced state
Bisphenol analogs analysis
SALDI-TOF MS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample Preparation for SALDI-TOF MS and Mass spectrometry analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The typical TEM image and size distribution of R-CDs; (B) UV–Vis spectrum, maximum excitation (λex) and emission (λem) spectra. The insets display R-CDs pictures under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) in aqueous solution; (C) FT-IR spectrum and (D) HR-XPS spectrum of the C 1s of R-CDs and inset shows the XPS spectrum.

SALDI-TOF MS spectra of BPA by utilization (A) CHCA matrix in positive (B) negative ion mode; (C) R-CD matrix in positive (D) negative ion mode. Asterisk represents background peak. The concentrations of BPA were adjusted to 1.0 μg mL−1.
Mass spectra of 0.5 μg mL−1 BPAF obtained by using R-CDs as matrix with different concentration. (Laser intensity of 65%).
Negative SALDI MS spectra of BPs by utilizing R-CDs and CDs as matrix, respectively: (A) BPB; (B) BPF; (C) BPS and (D) BPAF. The concentrations of BPs were adjusted to 1.0 μg mL−1.
(A) Schematic illustration of SALDI-TOF MS procedure based on R-CDs matrix; (B) Mass spectra of blank sewage; (C) 50 ng mL−1 BPs in spiked sewage by using R-CDs in negative ion mode.
The quantitative analysis of BPs of the proposed SALDI-TOF MS method.
Recoveries test of BPs in the sewage at two different spiked levels (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Reduced carbon nanodots as a novel substrate for direct analysis of bisphenol analogs in surface assisted laser desorption/ionization time of flight mass spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong, SAR

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Exchange student on visit to Hong Kong Baptist University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention in a variety of fields owing to the diversified properties. However, the control of the structure and surface function of CDs are still urgent issues to be addressed for using it in specific application. Herein, the reduced state of carbon nanodots (R-CDs) has been produced through a convenient strategy by reduction of the pure carbon dots products, which was utilized as a good candidate of matrix for direct analysis of bisphenol (BP) analogs in negative-ion surface-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (SALDI-TOF MS). Due to the dominant hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs, they were easier to absorb BPs through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions, resulting in highly efficient detection of BPs. Meanwhile, compared with the organic matrix and raw carbon dots, the superior surface structure and properties of this R-CDs surface provide various advantages including low background noise, outstanding sensitivity and salt tolerance. This work opens a door for developing R-CDs as alternative substrate in SALDI-TOF MS for direct detection of small molecule pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: As newly emerged carbon nano-materials, carbon dots (CDs) have presented enormous potential in a variety of fields owing to the diversified characteristics. For example, owing to the characters of simple tunable emission, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and easy functionalization, multifunctional CDs-based nano-systems have been developed and applied in chemosensors, bioimaging, photodynamic therapy, drug delivery and so on [1]. Due to the excellent electrical and optical properties, CDs are also active in photovoltaic devices, light emitting diodes, solar cells, microfluidic paper analytical devices, photodetectors and photocatalysts. They are able to enhance the performance of devices with simple methods and lesser cost [2–5]. However, among of the multitudinous applications, the new technology that combined CDs and other analysis or separation technologies such as mass spectrometry is still rare. Therefore, expanding the CDs’ application scope by adequately utilizing its advantages is a research hotspot.

In numerous mass spectrometry analytical techniques, matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) has displayed advantages such as simplicity, high speed, high throughput and the need of small sample volumes [6]. As an effective and powerful instrument for the analysis of different samples, especially complex biological samples, it has been widely used in various fields [7]. In the MALDI-TOF MS analysis, the choice of ideal substrate materials is essential for the analysis of target molecules because the matrix undertakes both as laser energy absorber and transmitter. But, the inherent ionization of the conventional organic matrices generates background interference in the low mass-to-charge (m/z) region (<1000), which obscures analyte peaks and complicates the detection of small molecules. To address this, matrix-free techniques have been developed with the name of surface-assisted laser desorption/ionization (SALDI). SALDI employs a nano-material on surface instead of a matrix, which improves the ionization efficiency, simplifies interpretation of spectrum, removes the extensive sample pre-treatments and thus becomes a more user friendly technique [8]. To date, various kinds of nanoparticles (NPs) have been widely applied for the detection of small molecules in SALDI-MS, owing to the effective binding or interaction with target molecules [9–14]. Carbon nano-materials are particularly attractive and have successfully detected a range of small molecules, due to its high conductivity, outstanding soft ionization capability, great light absorption and excellent energy transfer efficiency for SALDI [15–17]. Nevertheless, the complicated and cumbersome preparation process limit practical application of these methods. Beyond that, the low solubility and poor dispersibility of the pristine carbon materials always lead to unsatisfactory reproducibility [18]. Therefore, it is still necessary to explore suitable NPs for the application in SALDI-MS.

Bisphenols contain two phenol rings that are connected by different groups. Among these compounds, bisphenol A (BPA) as an intermediation in the production of polyvinyl chloride and epoxy plastics is widely applied in food or drink packagings [19–21]. BPA is not only exposed via the intake of food or liquid from the plastic containers, but also can be released into the aquatic environment via wastewater from the plastic-producing industrial plant. It has been reported that over one million pounds of BPA are released into the environment every year [22,23]. BPA as a famous endocrine-disruptor may lead to abnormal hormone activity and is thus associated with neurotoxicity, metabolic syndrome and even cancers [24,25]. In order to restrict the use of BPA, BPA analogs such as bisphenol B (BPB), bisphenol F (BPF), bisphenol S (BPS) and bisphenol AF (BPAF) are developed and used. But some of the related studies have shown that the analogs may have the potential toxicities similar to those of BPA. BPAF has been considered to be an even more potent ER agonist than BPA [26]. Due to the potential toxicity and health risk of BPs, analytical techniques including liquid chromatography- and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay and electrochemical biosensor have widely applied for BPs analysis [23,27–29], providing high detection sensitivity. However, they require time-consuming pre-treatment steps and are thus not time and cost efficient. Therefore, it is necessary to seek new approach with a simple and easy operation for the measurement of multiple BPs.

Herein, a reduced state carbon nanodots (R-CDs) was fabricated after reduction of the pure carbon dots products and applied for the analysis of bisphenol analogs by negative-ion mode SALDI-TOF MS. Significantly, the higher SALDI efficiency was observed for R-CDs because of its appropriate surface structure and good light absorption ability in the UV range. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface made R-CDs have excellent solubility and dispersibility, which avoided electric contamination and improved the experimental precision. As an ideal SALDI surface, the R-CDs in negative ion mode not only provided good sensitivity and salt tolerance for the analytes, but also possessed the simple and green synthesis and functionalization process. What's more, the optimal R-CDs were successfully employed the analysis of BPs in sewage samples.

The CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted pyrolysis method which is the same as the method in our previous work. [30] The precursor is oxalic acid. The R-CDs synthesized through reducing the pristine CDs by NaBH4. The detailed synthesis methods were described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample Preparation for SALDI-TOF MS and Mass spectrometry analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs and R-CDs solutions were prepared at concentration of 2.0 mg mL− 1 in water. CHCA (10 mg/mL) matrix was dissolved in ACN/H2O (2:1 v/v) containing 0.1% TFA. Each spotting was prepared by a matrix-first technique. A total of 1 μL of matrix solution was applied onto the sample plate and air-dried, followed by 1 μL analyte solution was pipetted on top of the matrix crystals. MALDI-TOF MS experiments were performed on a Bruker Autoflex Ⅱ mass spectrometer (Bruker Daltonics, Germany) equipped with a nitrogen laser operated at 337 nm and laser attenuator offset of ~65% in positive and negative reflection mode. The detailed instrument parameters and the method of sample treatment were described in the Supporting Information.

The R-CDs were obtained after direct reduction of pure carbon nanodots (CDs) which were synthesized by using oxalic acid as single precursor via microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Details of material synthesis and characterization were described in Supporting Information. The morphology and size of the R-CDs were illustrated in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. Fig. 1A clearly reveals that R-CDs are monodisperse with a narrow size distribution of 2.15–4.75 nm. The average diameter is 3.6 nm (Fig. 1A inset). In UV–Vis spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 1B), there is an apparent broad absorption peak from 300 nm to 340 nm. The range covered the most widely equipped lasers of MALDI-MS with 337 nm, which implied R-CDs as an appropriate receiver of energy could facilitate the subsequent ionization process. As shown in photoluminescence spectrum, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are located at 363 nm and 439 nm, respectively. They also possess good solubility in aqueous solution (left of inset) and emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation (right). The detailed composition and surface structure of the as-prepared R-CDs were surveyed through Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) measurement. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C) reveals oxygen related peaks on the surface of R-CDs such as the typical stretching vibrations of O‒H (3368 cm−1), C˭O (1711 cm−1), COO- (1587 cm−1), C‒O‒C (1072 cm−1), as well as the bending vibrations of O‒H (1453 cm−1) [31]. Similarly, the XPS spectrum shows only two peaks at 284.9 and 531.0 eV corresponding to C 1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1D inset). The high-resolved C 1s XPS spectrum also confirmed the formation of oxygen-containing groups including C˭O and C‒OH in the prepared R-CDs and hydroxyl groups were dominant after the reduction. The numerous hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs were featured for the dispersity and hydrophilicity of R-CDs in aqueous solutions. Moreover, they could effectively improve the reception and transfer of proton and electron, which suggested R-CDs could be a suitable matrix for SALDI-MS [32].

To verify the properties of the new matrix, the optical images of R-CDs with the analyte on MALDI target were compared with traditional matrix molecule α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA). As shown in Fig. S1, CHCA/analyte mixtures display the obvious heterogeneous co-crystallization, which will give rise to hot spots. In the sharp contrast, R-CDs matrix bypass this problem and express the outstanding homogenous distribution, which can promote a good shot-to-shot yield and enhance the accuracy of signal. In addition, the zoomed-in view of the spectra (Fig. S2A and B) showed a clean background and very low interference below the mass range of m/z 800 in either of the two modes when directly ionizing the R-CDs itself. However, many fragments and alkali metal adducts peaks of matrix appeared in the mass spectra of CHCA matrix alone in two ion modes (Fig. S2C and D). The results manifest that R-CDs are good candidates for the detection of the compounds with low mass in SALDI MS.

The performance of CHCA and R-CDs as matrices were evaluated with bisphenol A (BPA, MW = 228.29) as target small molecule for SALDI-TOF MS analysis in both ionization modes ( Fig. 2). When using CHCA as matrix in positive ion mode, no analyte signal was identified. Only the fragments and proton or alkali metal adducts peaks of matrix were detected (Fig. 2A). In negative ion mode, although the fragment [M–CH4–H]- ion of BPA was observed at m/z 211.11, the spectrum also was predominated by the strong background interferences from the matrix (Fig. 2C). When using R-CDs as matrix, however, the related ion peaks of BPA were detected including [M + Na]+ at m/z 251.10 in positive ion mode (Fig. 2B), [M–H]− at m/z 227.12 and [M–CH4–H]− at m/z 211.10 in the negative ion spectrum (Fig. 2B and D, the fragmentation pathway in Supporting Information). Moreover, it was observed that the characteristic peaks of BPA reflected higher S/N ratio with a relatively clear background in negative ion mode. The results manifested that negative ion mode was the preferable option to directly analyze BPA with R-CDs matrix.

In order to minimize the signal attenuation caused by the matrix itself and get the optimized mass signal response, we examined the influence of the different R-CDs matrix concentrations to signal intensity in negative ion mode using bisphenol AF (BPAF, MW = 336.23) as a model compound ( Fig. 3). The signal intensity of [M-H]- ion of BPAF with the concentration of R-CDs matrix addition from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 5.0 mg mL−1 increased first, then declined gradually. Thus, the concentration of R-CDs with 2.0 mg mL−1 was set as the best condition for the further analysis. The precision and effectiveness of the instrumental analysis were studied by evaluating the MS signals across different locations and spots on the target surface with R-CDs matrix for detection of bisphenol S (BPS, MW = 250.27). The signal intensities were stabilized for either spot-to-spot (Fig. S4) or different locations in a single spot (Fig. S5). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 8.06% (n = 10) and 7.41% (n = 15), respectively.

Furthermore, other bisphenol analogs such as bisphenol B (BPB, MW = 242.32) and bisphenol F (BPF, MW = 200.24) were also analyzed using the same procedure. As displayed in Fig. 4A−D, the four BPs were distinctly identified by employing R-CDs as matrix in negative ion SALDI MS. As shown in Fig. 4A, the MS peaks were attributed to the [M–H]− (m/z 241.12), [M–CH4 −H]− (m/z 225.09), and [M– C2H6–H]− (m/z 211.07) ions for BPB. Additionally, the deprotonated ions of BPF, BPS and BPAF were detected at m/z 199.08, 249.02 and 335.06, respectively, with nearly free background interference. It was worth noting that the pure CDs matrix gave poor mass spectra with lower signal intensities than R-CDs under the same condition. Such good performance of R-CDs was attributed to the dominant hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs after reduction. As shown in Table S1, compared with pristine CDs, the percentages of hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs increased appreciably from 11.96% to 18.65% after the reduction, and carbonyl groups decreased from 21.35% to 13.79%. According to previous reports [32], hydroxyl-group-dominated graphite dots could enhance structural stability of matrix and effectively decreased background noise caused by local defects of matrix structure through energy-favorable pathways for the dissociation of carbon-free groups in negative ion mode. Therefore, in the current case, the outstanding results may infer that the target molecular BPs contained multiple hydroxyl groups. Through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions, the hydroxyl groups in BPs were easily adsorbed onto the surface of hydroxyl-group-dominated R-CDs. Under the laser irradiation, the R-CDs might rapidly transfer the energy via the dissociation for hydroxyl species and capture protons in the target analytes, which provided good sensitivity ( Fig. 5A). The results verified again that the use of R-CDs as matrix in negative ion mode SALDI MS exhibited unique benefits.

To further demonstrate the sensitivity of R-CDs assisted LDI-TOF MS analysis, mass spectra of BPs with different content were examined. As presented in Fig. S6 and Table 1, the good linearity with R2 better than 0.9850 for the five BPs. The instrument detection limits of the BPs and the limits of quantification were achieved in range of 0.45–5.08 ng mL−1 and 1.50–16.93 ng mL−1 accordingly. The results obtained in the present work are comparable to other previously reported analytical methods for the detection of BPs (Table S2). Beyond that, there are high-concentration salt in the real sample usually, which may directly affect the ionization efficiency and give rise to lower signal-to-noise ratio [33]. Thus, the salt tolerance of the optimized matrix R-CDs was evaluated by adding NaCl solution to BPS standards with concentration ranging from 10–1000 mM (Fig. S7). The signal intensity of BPS decreased about 12%, with the initial addition of 10 mM NaCl. The signals tended to be stable when the salt concentration increased up to 1000 mM, which implied that R-CDs matrix possessed robust salt tolerance.

Based on the above performance, the R-CDs was applied for the measurement of bisphenols in sewage. Fig. 5B and C exhibited the mass spectra of blank sewage and the sample with addition of five BPs. Some unknown ion peaks were observed with low S/N ratios in spectrum of blank sample. On the other hand, characteristic deprotonated ion peaks [M–H]− of five BPs and the [M–CH4–H]− ions for BPA were obtained distinctly in the sample spiked with 50 ng mL−1 five BPs. Besides, when separately spiking five BPs with two levels (50 and 200 ng mL−1) in sewage, recoveries were from 92.8% to 102.6% with RSDs of 3.6–6.2% ( Table 2), respectively, suggesting that R-CDs have great potential to be used for the analysis of bisphenol analogs in real samples with SALDI MS technique.

In conclusion, we have synthesized the reduced carbon nanodots through a green and convenient strategy and utilized it as a novel candidate for detection of bisphenol analogs in negative ion mode SALDI-TOF MS. The R-CDs-assisted LDI MS exhibited excellent performance including extremely low background noise, good precision and high sensitivity in analysis of BPs compared with the organic matrix CHCA and CDs, because the dominant hydroxyl groups on the surface of R-CDs were easier to absorb BPs through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions, and promoted the highly efficient energy and proton transfer process. In addition, the method applicability was demonstrated with the analysis of BPs in spiked sewage samples by employing the optimal R-CDs surface. The results indicate that the R-CDs surface could achieve high sensitivity for the analysis in real samples with SALDI MS technique. The R-CDs-assisted LDI MS may be promising for its extension for monitoring other environmental pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475080, 21575084 and 91543202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.07.062.
